items.title: Use plain language and avoid figures of speech, idioms, and
items.description: Write at an 8th-grade reading level. Avoid
items.whyItMatters: Plain language reduces cognitive load, benefiting users with cognitive disabilities, non-native speakers, and readers who are stressed or multi-tasking.
items.bad: It's a no-brainer that we'll move the needle and circle back once we've boiled the ocean on this synergistic, paradigm-shifting deliverable.
items.good: This is a simple choice. We'll work on it together and update you next week.

items.title: Make sure that button , a , and label element content is unique
items.description: Button, <a>, and <label> content must be
items.whyItMatters: Descriptive link text provides context to keyboard and screen reader users, allowing them to browse a list of links without needing to read the surrounding text.
items.bad: Three identical links: Read more · Click here · Learn more
items.good: Read the 2025 accessibility report , Download the WCAG quick-reference PDF , View pricing for the Pro plan

items.title: Use left-aligned text for left-to-right (LTR) languages, and right-aligned
items.description: Use left-aligned text for LTR languages. Never
items.whyItMatters: Justified or centered body text is highly difficult to read for people with reading disabilities like dyslexia, as it creates uneven spacing and visual rivers of white space.
items.bad:
{4-sentence Lorem ipsum}

— rivers of whitespace visible.
items.good: {same text}

items.title: Validate your HTML
items.description: Write valid HTML. No unclosed tags, duplicate IDs,
items.whyItMatters: Valid HTML guarantees that assistive technologies can parse and navigate the page's DOM tree reliably and consistently across all standard browsers.
items.bad:
Hello world

  • orphan
    • — render via dangerouslySetInnerHTML so the malformed structure persists.
items.good:
Hello world

  • list item

items.title: Use a lang attribute on the html element
items.description: Always include lang attribute on <html>. Example:
items.whyItMatters: The lang attribute allows screen readers to choose the correct voice engine, phonetic profiles, and pronunciation rules to announce content accurately.
items.bad:
<html>
block showing missing lang.
items.good: <html lang="en">

items.title: Provide a unique title for each page or view
items.description: Every page/view must have a unique <title>
items.whyItMatters: Page titles are the first things announced by screen readers when navigating. They allow users to quickly identify if they have reached their desired destination.
items.bad: Mock browser tab bar with three tabs all reading "Untitled".
items.good: Three tabs reading "Pricing — Acme", "Contact — Acme", "Docs — Acme".

items.title: Ensure that viewport zoom is not disabled
items.description: Never use maximum-scale=1 or userscalable=no in viewport meta.
items.whyItMatters: Disabling viewport scaling locks out visually impaired users who depend on browser or mobile zoom to read small text and interact with layouts.
items.bad:
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initialscale=1, maximum-scale=1, user-scalable=no">
.
items.good: <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initialscale=1">

items.title: Use landmark elements to indicate important content regions. WCAG
items.description: Use <header>, <nav>, <main>, <footer>, <aside> —
items.whyItMatters: Landmark elements (header, nav, main, footer) allow screen reader users to skip directly to major layout regions, bypassing repetitive content.
items.bad:
…
…
…
rendered as a visual mockup with
labels.
items.good: Same mockup using <header> , <nav> , <main> , <footer>

items.title: Avoid using tabindex values other than 0 or -1
items.description: Never use tabindex values other than 0 or -1.
items.whyItMatters: Natural DOM order ensures keyboard focus flows logically. Modifying the order with positive tabindex values creates jumbled navigation that disorients users.
items.bad:
Four buttons with tabIndex={4} {2} {1} {3} so Tab order is jumbled vs visual
order.
items.good: Four buttons with no tabIndex ; natural DOM order.

items.title: Avoid using the autofocus attribute
items.description: Never use the autofocus attribute on inputs.
items.whyItMatters: Autofocus disorients screen reader and low-vision users by moving focus without warning, bypassing important introductory content on page load.
items.bad:
A "Click to demo" button reveals <input autoFocus placeholder="Search"/> (gate
via click so it can't steal real page focus on initial load).
items.good: Same input without autofocus, with a clear <label>Search</label>

items.title: Allow extending session timeouts
items.description: Any session timeout must show a visible
items.whyItMatters: People with motor, reading, or cognitive difficulties need more time to complete actions. Warn them before logging out so they do not lose progress.
items.bad: Panel showing "You will be logged out in 0:14 …" countdown and no controls.
items.good: Same countdown plus a "Need more time? Extend session (+15 min)" button.

items.title: Remove title attribute tooltips
items.description: Never use the title attribute as the only label
items.whyItMatters: The title attribute tooltip is inaccessible to keyboard-only and mobile touch users. Important label text must be visible or accessible via screen readers.
items.bad: <button title="Save your work"> </button> (icon-only, info hidden in title ).
items.good: with visible label, or visually-hidden text via sr-only class.

items.title: Make sure there is a visible focus style for interactive elements that
items.description: Never set outline:none or outline:0 on
items.whyItMatters: Visible focus indicators are like a mouse cursor for keyboard users. Removing them makes it impossible to know which button or input has active focus.
items.bad:
<button className="focus:outline-none">…</button> plus a CSS outline:none
rule.
items.good: <button className="focus-visible:outline focus-visible:outline-2 focusvisible:outline-offset-2 focus-visible:outline-blue-600">…</button>

items.title: Check to see that keyboard focus order matches the visual
items.description: Tab order must match visual reading
items.whyItMatters: When visual layout and tabbing order do not align, keyboard users will tab to unexpected regions, causing severe confusion and disorientation.
items.bad:
A two-column grid where DOM order is [Right1, Left1, Right2, Left2] (use
Tailwind order- utilities) so Tab jumps right→left.
items.good: Same visual but DOM order matches reading order.

items.title: Remove invisible focusable elements
items.description: Elements hidden off-screen (menus,
items.whyItMatters: If interactive controls hidden off-screen (like hamburger menus) can still receive keyboard focus, users get trapped in a hidden, confusing tab loop.
items.bad: Off-screen menu hidden by transform: translateX(-9999px) but still in tab order.
items.good: Same menu hidden with hidden attribute or inert , so Tab skips it.

items.title: Make sure that all img elements have an alt attribute
items.description: Every must have an alt attribute.
items.whyItMatters: Screen readers read alt text aloud to visually impaired users. Without alt text, the browser reads the file name, which is unhelpful and confusing.
items.bad: (no alt — screen reader will announce the filename).

items.title: Make sure that decorative images use null alt (empty)
items.description: Decorative images must use alt="" and
items.whyItMatters: Empty alt attributes (alt="") signal to screen readers that an image is decorative and should be skipped entirely, reducing verbal noise.
items.bad: between two paragraphs.
items.good: Same divider with alt="" and role="presentation"

items.title: Provide a text alternative for complex images such as charts
items.description: Charts, graphs, and maps must have a
items.whyItMatters: Complex visual data like charts, maps, and infographics cannot be summarized in a simple sentence. Providing a structured data table ensures full accessibility.
items.bad: A bar-chart only.
items.good:
Same chart inside <figure> ; below it a <figcaption> plus a <table> with the
underlying data.

items.title: For images containing text, make sure the alt description includes the
items.description: Alt text for images containing text must include
items.whyItMatters: Any text embedded within an image is completely invisible to screen readers, translation tools, and text-resizing tools unless reproduced in the alt description.
items.bad: .

items.title: Use heading elements to introduce content
items.description: Use <h1>–<h6> for headings. Never use
items.whyItMatters: Proper heading elements organize content semantically, allowing screen reader users to navigate the document structure easily and skip directly to sections.
items.bad:
Pricing

(visually a heading, semantically a paragraph).
items.good: Pricing

items.title: Use only one h1 element per page or view
items.description: Only one <h1> per page.
items.whyItMatters: Using a single H1 acts as the clear title for the page, orienting screen reader users and defining the core theme of the page for SEO and accessibility.
items.bad: Mock page with three <h1> elements.
items.good: One <h1> followed by <h2> subsections.

items.title: Heading elements should be written in a logical sequence
items.description: Heading levels must follow logical
items.whyItMatters: Consistent, descending heading levels (H1, H2, H3) provide a clear outline that maps the logical relationships between page topics.
items.bad:
<h1> → <h4> → <h2> → <h6> displayed in the demo as an outline tree with red flags
on out-of-order nodes.
items.good: <h1> → <h2> → <h3> → <h2> with green check tree.

items.title: Don't skip heading levels
items.description: Never skip a heading level (e.g. h2 directly
items.whyItMatters: Skipping heading levels (like jumping from H2 directly to H4) implies missing sections, confusing screen reader users who navigate by heading outline.
items.bad: <h2> directly followed by <h4> .

items.title: Use list elements ( ol , ul , and dl elements) for list content. WCAG
items.description: Use <ul>, <ol>, or <dl> for all list content.
items.whyItMatters: Semantic lists inform assistive technology of grouped items and the total count of items, helping users mentally structure the content.
items.bad:
• Apples
• Pears
• Plums
.
items.good:
• Apples
  • Pears
  • Plums

items.title: Use the a element for links
items.description: Use <a href="..."> for navigation. Never use
items.whyItMatters: Links are built for navigation. Standard anchor tags provide essential keyboard features like Enter activation, status bar hover, and history tracking.
items.bad: navigate('/x')} className="text-blue-600 underline cursor-pointer">Pricing .
items.good: Pricing

items.title: Ensure that links are recognizable as links
items.description: Links must be distinguishable from body text by
items.whyItMatters: People with low vision or colorblindness cannot distinguish links from standard text if color is the only cue. Underlines ensure they stand out visually.
items.bad:
A paragraph where links are colored blue but have no underline; toggle the demo to
grayscale to show they vanish.
items.good: Same paragraph where links are colored AND underlined.

items.title: Ensure that controls have :focus states
items.description: All interactive controls must have a
items.whyItMatters: A clear, prominent focus indicator is vital for keyboard navigation, allowing users to know exactly where they are on the page.
items.bad: Three buttons with outline-none .
items.good: Three buttons with visible focus-visible:ring-2 ring-blue-600 ring-offset-2

items.title: Use the button element for buttons
items.description: Use <button> for all clickable actions.
items.whyItMatters: Standard buttons provide built-in Space and Enter activation. Faking buttons with div elements locks out keyboard-only and screen reader users.
items.bad:
Submit
(no Enter/Space activation, not in tab order without tabindex).
items.good: <button type="button" onClick={…}>Submit</button>

items.title: Provide a skip link and make sure that it is visible when focused
items.description: Every page must have a skip link as the first focusable
items.whyItMatters: Skip links allow keyboard-only and screen reader users to bypass long header navigation lists and jump directly to the primary page content.
items.bad: Mock page with no skip link; tabbing forces user through 30 nav items.
items.good: First focusable element: Skip to main content

items.title: Identify links that open in a new tab or window. G201 — Technique (no
items.wcagTitle: Give users a choice of whether to open links in a new window/tab
items.description: Links opening in a new tab must warn the
items.whyItMatters: Opening a new tab without warning can disorient screen reader and low-vision users who might not realize the browser focus has moved to a new window.
items.bad: Read more .
items.good: Read more (opens in new tab)

items.title: Use the table element to describe tabular data
items.description: Use <table> for tabular data. Never use CSS Grid
items.whyItMatters: Semantic tables expose columns, rows, and cells to screen readers, allowing users to navigate and understand complex data points systematically.
items.bad: A grid built from CSS Grid with <div> cells.
items.good:
Same data in …
…

items.title: Use the th element for table headers (with appropriate scope
items.description: Table headers must use <th scope="col"> or <th
items.whyItMatters: The scope attribute explicitly connects header cells to their corresponding data rows or columns, ensuring correct reading order in grid structures.
items.bad: Table where the header row uses <td> cells.
items.good:
Table with <th scope="col"> on column headers and <th scope="row"> on row
headers.

items.title: Use the caption element to provide a title for the table
items.description: Every data table must have a <caption> element.
items.whyItMatters: A table caption acts as a visible heading for the table, giving assistive technology users instant context about the table data before reading it cell by cell.
items.bad: Table with a <h3> above it acting as title.
items.good:
…
Q3 2025 sales by region

items.title: All inputs in a form are associated with a corresponding label
items.description: Every input must have an associated <label>
items.whyItMatters: Labels provide persistent descriptions. They stay visible when typing and are read automatically by screen readers when focusing on an input field.
items.bad: <input placeholder="Email"/> only.
items.good: <label htmlFor="email">Email</label><input id="email" type="email"/>

items.title: Use fieldset and legend elements where appropriate
items.description: Group related inputs (radio, checkbox) in
items.whyItMatters: Fieldset and legend elements group related options, such as radio groups or address forms, providing essential shared context for screen reader users.
items.bad:
Group of three radio buttons with a

"Shipping speed" above them.
items.good: <fieldset><legend>Shipping speed</legend>…three radios…</fieldset>

items.title: Inputs use autocomplete where appropriate
items.description: Personal data inputs must have autocomplete
items.whyItMatters: The autocomplete attribute speeds up entry and reduces typing requirements, helping users with motor and cognitive difficulties fill forms easily.
items.bad: <input name="firstname"/> with no autocomplete .
items.good:
<input name="firstname" autoComplete="given-name"/> plus address fields with
autoComplete="street-address" , "postal-code" , etc. Testparty

items.title: Make sure that form input errors are displayed in list above the form
items.description: On form submit failure, show an error summary
items.whyItMatters: An error summary lists all form issues in one place, focused on submit failure. This allows users to review and navigate to fields needing correction.
items.bad: Inline red text under each invalid field, no summary.
items.good:
2 errors

  • Email is required
  • ZIP must be 5 digits
above the form, focused on submit-fail.

items.title: Associate input error messaging with the input it corresponds
items.description: Associate error messages with inputs using
items.whyItMatters: Associating error text with fields using aria-describedby ensures screen readers automatically announce the specific error message when focusing a field.
items.bad:
Email is required

.
items.good:
Email is required

. Testparty

items.title: Make sure that error, warning, and success states are not visually
items.description: Error, warning, and success states must use
items.whyItMatters: Color blind or visually impaired users cannot identify validation states (like red borders) unless supported by clear text labels or descriptive icons.
items.bad: Three inputs differentiated only by red/green border.
items.good:
Same inputs with <AlertCircle/> / <CheckCircle/> icons + text labels ("Error: …",
"Success: …").

items.title: Make sure that media does not autoplay
items.description: Never autoplay audio or video. Always use controls and
items.whyItMatters: Autoplay audio can drown out screen reader speech, making it extremely difficult for visually impaired users to find controls to turn it off.
items.bad:
<audio src="/clip.mp3" autoPlay loop/> (gate behind a "Run demo" button so we
don't actually autoplay on page load).
items.good: <audio src="/clip.mp3" controls preload="none"/>

items.title: Ensure that media controls use appropriate markup
items.description: Media controls must use semantic HTML:
items.whyItMatters: Using standard, keyboard-accessible buttons for custom media players guarantees that all play, pause, and volume controls are fully functional for all users.
items.bad:
for mute;
…
for volume.
items.good:
<button aria-pressed={muted} onClick={…}>{muted?'Unmute':'Mute'}
</button> ; <input type="range" min="0" max="100" aria-label="Volume"/>

items.title: Check to see that all media can be paused
items.description: All moving or auto-updating content must be
items.whyItMatters: Any moving, blinking, or scrolling content must be pausable to avoid triggering seizure issues, distracting users, or blocking assistive technologies.
items.bad: A looping <video autoPlay loop muted> with no controls.
items.good: Same video with controls and a visible "Pause all media" global button.

items.title: Confirm the presence of captions
items.description: All prerecorded video must have captions via
items.whyItMatters: Captions are essential for deaf or hard-of-hearing users to access verbal dialogue and ambient sounds, while also helping non-native language learners.
items.bad: <video src="/talk.mp4" controls/> with no <track> .
items.good:
<video controls><source src="/talk.mp4"/><track kind="captions"
srcLang="en" src="/talk.vtt" default/></video>

items.title: Remove seizure triggers
items.description: Never produce content that flashes more than 3
items.whyItMatters: Strobe effects and fast flashing lights can trigger epileptic seizures. High-frequency animations must be avoided or strictly gated.
items.bad:
Render a STILL image with overlay text "Imagine this strobing at 5 Hz red/white"
gated behind a "Show simulated bad demo (warning: flashing)" button with a confirm
dialog. Do NOT actually exceed 3 flashes/sec on render — the spec is unambiguous on
this.
items.good: A subtle 1 Hz pulse with prefers-reduced-motion disabling it entirely.

items.title: Confirm that transcripts are available
items.description: All audio content must have a text transcript
items.whyItMatters: Transcripts provide access to audio content for deaf-blind users, users who prefer reading, and those in noisy environments where audio is impractical.
items.bad: <audio controls src="/podcast.mp3"/> alone.
items.good:
Same audio +
Transcript
…full text…
below.

items.title: Check your content in specialized browsing modes. WCAG
items.description: Interactive elements must remain
items.whyItMatters: Using system-color support ensures controls remain fully visible and usable in specialized layouts, such as Windows High Contrast Mode.
items.bad:
A button styled with background-image: linear-gradient(...) and no border —
disappears in Windows High Contrast Mode.
items.good: Same button with explicit border: 1px solid currentColor and CSS systemcolor fallback ( background-color: ButtonFace; color: ButtonText; ).

items.title: Increase text size to 200%
items.description: Layout must not break or clip content when text
items.whyItMatters: Visually impaired users often scale browser text to 200%. Layouts must reflow cleanly without cutting off, overlapping, or hiding enlarged text content.
items.bad:
A card with width: 200px; height: 60px; overflow: hidden so doubled text clips.
Demo includes a "Zoom 200%" toggle.
items.good: Same card with min-height and overflow: visible ; doubled text reflows.

items.title: Double-check that good proximity between content is
items.description: Labels must be visually close to the inputs
items.whyItMatters: Placing labels or related descriptions far away from inputs disorients screen magnifier users, who can only view a small portion of the screen at once.
items.bad: A label on the far left and its input on the far right of a wide row.
items.good: Label sits directly above its input.

items.title: Make sure color isn't the only way information is conveyed. WCAG
items.description: Never use color alone to convey state or
items.whyItMatters: Relying on color alone to convey details (like active pages or statuses) locks out users with color blindness, low vision, or monochrome monitors.
items.bad: A list of files where "errors" are red and "passing" are green, no other indicator.
items.good:
Same list with <XCircle/> / <CheckCircle/> icons and "Error" / "Passing" text
labels.

items.title: Make sure instructions are not visual or audio-only. WCAG
items.description: Instructions must name the element they
items.whyItMatters: Instructions must not rely on single sensory indicators (like 'click the red circle' or 'the button on the right') which are useless to blind users.
items.bad:
Click the green button on the right to continue.

.
items.good:
Click the green "Continue" button to proceed.

(names the element).

items.title: Use a simple, straightforward, and consistent layout
items.description: Layout must reflow to a single column at 320px
items.whyItMatters: Clean, logical, single-column reflow patterns ensure readability and ease of interaction across various screen widths and device magnifiers.
items.bad: A demo with floating sidebars, overlapping cards, three nav bars.
items.good: Clean single-column with one nav, one main, one footer.

items.title: Ensure animations are subtle and do not flash too much
items.description: Animations must not flash more than 3 times/sec.
items.whyItMatters: Loud, flashing, or fast-scaling animations can trigger visual vertigo, nausea, and severe distraction in users with vestibular disorders.
items.bad: A box that scales 0.5→2.0 and shakes 10°/100ms.
items.good: A box with a 200ms ease fade-in.

items.title: Provide a mechanism to pause background video
items.description: Background video must have a pause control.
items.whyItMatters: Moving backgrounds draw strong cognitive attention. Users with ADHD or vestibular disorders must be able to pause background videos to read text.
items.bad: Hero section with <video autoPlay loop muted/> and no pause control.
items.good:
Same hero with a small <button aria-pressed={paused}>Pause
background</button> overlay.

items.title: Make sure all animation obeys the prefers-reduced-motion
items.description: All CSS animations must respect: @media
items.whyItMatters: The prefers-reduced-motion query allows users with vestibular motion sensitivity to disable transitions, parallax effects, and spinning animations.
items.bad: A spinning gear forever — animation has no media-query guard.
items.good:
Same gear with @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { .gear {
animation: none; } }

items.title: Check the contrast for all normal-sized text
items.description: Normal text (<18px or <14px bold) must have
items.whyItMatters: Sufficient color contrast (minimum 4.5:1) ensures standard body text is legible for users with moderate low vision, color blindness, or in high-glare setups.
items.bad: (≈2.5:1).
items.good: (≈19:1).

items.title: Check the contrast for all large-sized text
items.description: Large text (≥18px or ≥14px bold) must have
items.whyItMatters: Large text needs a minimum of 3:1 contrast ratio. This standard ensures high legibility while offering creative flexibility for page headers and callouts.
items.bad:
Heading

.
items.good: Heading

items.title: Check the contrast for all icons
items.description: UI icons must have ≥3:1 contrast against their
items.whyItMatters: UI controls, status indicators, and descriptive icons must maintain high contrast (3:1) so their functions are clearly recognizable.
items.bad: Lucide <Search/> at text-zinc-300 on white.
items.good: Same icon at text-zinc-700

items.title: Check the contrast of borders for input elements (text input
items.description: Input borders must have ≥3:1 contrast
items.whyItMatters: High-contrast input borders (3:1) allow low-vision and cognitively impaired users to easily locate, outline, and focus form fields.
items.bad: <input className="border border-zinc-200"/> .
items.good: <input className="border border-zinc-500"/>

items.title: Check text that overlaps images or video
items.description: Text overlaid on images must meet contrast
items.whyItMatters: Busy photographic backgrounds make overlaid text illegible. Using dark scrims or solid backing plates ensures text meets safety contrast margins.
items.bad: White text directly on a busy photo.
items.good:
Same photo with a <div className="bg-black/60 absolute inset-0"/> scrim or
text in a solid pill.

items.title: Check custom ::selection colors
items.description: Custom ::selection styles must maintain ≥4.5:1
items.whyItMatters: Custom text selection states must retain high contrast, ensuring selected text remains fully readable against the custom highlight color.
items.bad: A <style> block injecting ::selection { background: #fff5; color: #fff } .
items.good: ::selection { background: #1e40af; color: #fff }

items.title: Check that the site can be rotated to any orientation
items.description: Never lock orientation. Content must work in
items.whyItMatters: Locking the screen orientation to portrait or landscape prevents users on fixed mounts (like wheelchairs) from reading or interacting with the site.
items.bad:
A demo card with CSS @media (orientation: portrait) { .lock { display:
block } } showing a "Please rotate your device" overlay.
items.good: Same card without the lock; content reflows for both orientations.

items.title: Remove horizontal scrolling
items.description: No horizontal scrolling at 320px viewport
items.whyItMatters: Avoiding horizontal scroll at standard widths ensures content wraps vertically, allowing users of screen magnifiers and mobile devices to read smoothly.
items.bad:
A <div style={{width:'1200px'}}> inside a 320px container forcing horizontal
scroll.
items.good: Same content with max-width:100% and flex-wrap:wrap

items.title: Ensure that button and link icons can be activated with ease
items.description: Touch targets must be ≥44×44px with adequate
items.whyItMatters: A minimum touch target size of 44x44px ensures people with tremors, motor impairments, or large fingers can activate controls without error.
items.bad: Three icon buttons each 18×18px crammed together.
items.good: Three icon buttons each min-h-[44px] min-w-[44px] with gap-2

items.title: Ensure sufficient space between interactive items in order to
items.description: Interactive elements must have enough spacing
items.whyItMatters: Adequate spacing around touch zones prevents accidental triggers and provides motor-impaired users a safe area to swipe and scroll.
items.bad: A vertical list of full-width buttons with gap-0 — no scroll dead-zone.
items.good:
Same list with gap-3 plus margin so a tremor-affected user can scroll between
them.
7. Final implementation notes
Single JSX file: Put CHECKLIST in the same file. With 64 entries × ~3 lines of metadata +
two small functional components per entry, expect roughly 1,200–1,500 LOC. Acceptable
for a demo.
Lucide icons used: CheckCircle2 , XCircle , AlertCircle , ExternalLink , Search ,
Save , Volume2 , VolumeX , Filter , RotateCcw , plus the category icons listed in §1.
Sticky-but-accessible header: Don't trap focus; the skip link demo (#29) must be the first
focusable element on the actual page (eat-your-own-dog-food).
Safety guards on demos: The flashing demo (#44) must NOT actually flash above 3 Hz on
render; gate "Show flashing demo" behind a confirm and render a static image with overlay
caption. The autoplay audio (#40) must be muted by default and gated behind a button.
The autofocus demo (#10) must be gated so it can't steal page focus on initial mount.
Persisting progress: localStorage key a11y-checklist-done (JSON array of IDs).
Print stylesheet: @media print { .demo-surface { box-shadow: none } aside {
display: none } } .

Recommendations
1. Build in this order: (1) data array with empty badDemo / goodDemo placeholders → (2)
layout shell + filters + progress → (3) fill in badDemo / goodDemo for one category at a time,
starting with Controls and Forms (highest visual variety, most pedagogical value). Ship
after each category.
2. Test the app against itself. Run axe DevTools and a screen reader (NVDA on Windows,
VoiceOver on macOS) on the finished build before merging — the app fails its own purpose
if it has any A or AA violations in its chrome. Threshold: zero axe-detected violations at AA
before merging to main.
3. Threshold to add a "Print as PDF audit checklist" feature: ship only after all 16 categories
render and pass internal axe scans. Threshold to add per-team progress sync
(Supabase/Firebase): only if more than one user requests it; localStorage covers solo use.
4. Threshold to upgrade target to WCAG 2.2 AA-strict: when you remove items #4 and #32
from the data (or display them only as historical/educational), since their underlying SC
(4.1.1 Parsing) is obsolete in WCAG 2.2. Until then, surface the obsolete state in the UI rather
than pretending it still counts.

5. License: Credit "Checklist content adapted from The A11Y Project (APLv2,
https://www.a11yproject.com/checklist/)" in the footer; APLv2 requires attribution but
allows derivative works.

Caveats
The A11Y Project page does not display A/AA/AAA levels per item; the levels in §6 come
from cross-referencing each item's cited success criterion against the W3C WCAG 2.2
Recommendation. If a11yproject.com swaps an item's WCAG link (e.g. retiring a 4.1.1
reference in favor of 1.3.1), update the data file accordingly.
4.1.1 Parsing (used for items #4 "Validate your HTML" and #32 <th scope> ) is

obsolete in WCAG 2.2. The W3C says verbatim: "This criterion has been removed from
WCAG 2.2." (https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/parsing.html). Keep the
items, mark them obsolete in the badge, and link to the W3C parsing-removal note.
Implementers should still perform the underlying check (validity helps assistive tech
indirectly), but it no longer counts toward formal WCAG 2.2 conformance. W3C
G201 is a Technique, not a Success Criterion — there is no required conformance level for

"warn before opening new window". The A11Y Project itself flags this verbatim: "While this
technique is technically not required for compliance, it is an often-cited area of frustration
for many different kinds of assistive technology users."
Item count is 64, taken directly from the live page on May 7, 2026. If the A11Y Project edits
the checklist (the project is an active GitHub repo at a11yproject/a11yproject.com),
regenerate the array from the source markdown.
The "8th grade reading level" target in item #1 is the A11Y Project's own framing; the W3C
WCAG 2.2 SC 3.1.5 itself uses "lower secondary education level" — they are roughly
equivalent, but the description in the data should be quoted verbatim as the A11Y Project
wrote it. W3C W3C
Some demos (#44 seizure triggers, #40 autoplay, #10 autofocus) have safety/UX
implications. The bad demo must SIMULATE the failure (still image with overlay text
"Imagine this strobing at 5 Hz") rather than actually trigger it on render. Build these last so
QA can verify the gating works before any real user encounters them.
